intro.overview: This week begins your cryptography training at NEXUS Security. You’ll master the fundamental cryptographic operations that underpin modern security: hashing for integrity verification, symmetric encryption for data protection, asymmetric encryption for secure key exchange, and digital signatures for non-repudiation. These skills prepare you for the Gulf Coast Healthcare engagement in Week 3, where you’ll remediate certificate issues on their patient portal.

mentorQuote.title: Security Compliance Specialist, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Cryptography isn’t magic — it’s math. And math done wrong is worse than no math at all because it gives false confidence. Every healthcare organization I’ve audited had encryption somewhere. Most had it implemented incorrectly. This week, you’ll learn to do it right.

focusAreas.title: Cryptographic Hashing
focusAreas.description: SHA-256 and the avalanche effect — how small input changes produce completely different outputs, forming the foundation of data integrity verification.

focusAreas.title: Symmetric Encryption
focusAreas.description: AES-256-CBC encryption and decryption — the HIPAA-required standard for protecting PHI at rest.

focusAreas.title: Asymmetric Encryption
focusAreas.description: RSA keypair generation and public-key encryption — solving the key distribution problem that symmetric encryption cannot.

focusAreas.title: Digital Signatures
focusAreas.description: Creating and verifying signatures using RSA — providing non-repudiation, the cryptographic proof that a specific party signed a specific document.

focusAreas.title: Algorithm Deprecation
focusAreas.description: Why MD5 and SHA-1 are no longer acceptable — understanding collision resistance and NIST recommendations for current practice.

focusAreas.title: Documentation for Auditors
focusAreas.description: Every screenshot and command output is evidence. Building the habit of capturing before-and-after proof prepares you for real compliance engagements.

objectives: Generate and compare cryptographic hashes using SHA-256
Encrypt and decrypt files using AES symmetric encryption
Generate RSA keypairs and perform asymmetric encryption
Document all operations for your Lab 2.3 Engagement Packet

callouts.title: Prerequisite
callouts.text: This lab requires a working Kali Linux VM from Lab 1.1. If you haven’t completed Lab 1.1, stop here and complete it first.

callouts.title: Save For Lab 2.3
callouts.text: This is Week 1 of a 3-week module. Create a folder called Module2_Screenshots and save all screenshots there. You’ll need them for your Engagement Packet in Week 3.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: This is Week 1 of a 3-week module. Time here is internal NEXUS training — not yet billable to Gulf Coast Healthcare. Create a Module2_Screenshots folder now and save all 9 screenshots there. You will compile everything into a single Engagement Packet in Lab 2.3.

content.whatYoureDoing: Generating cryptographic hashes using SHA-256 and demonstrating the avalanche effect — how small input changes produce completely different outputs.
content.whyItMatters: Hashes are the foundation of data integrity. Every time you verify a software download, check file integrity, or validate a digital signature, you’re using hashes. In healthcare, hash verification proves that patient records haven’t been tampered with — critical for audit trails and legal compliance.

content.title: Security Compliance Specialist, NEXUS Security
content.quote: Auditors don’t care what you did. They care what you can prove you did. Hash verification is how you prove data integrity.

content.part: Part A: Create a test file and generate its hash

steps.text: Create a sample patient record file:
steps.command: echo "Patient: John Smith, DOB: 1985-03-15, MRN: 12345" > patient.txt

whatThisDoes.concept: sha256sum computes a SHA-256 cryptographic hash — a fixed-length, 64-character hexadecimal fingerprint of a file. The same input always produces the same hash, but even a one-bit change produces a completely different output. This is how you prove a file has not been tampered with.
whatThisDoes.output: You should see a 64-character hex string followed by the filename. This hash is unique to your file’s exact contents. Copy it somewhere — you’ll compare it in the next step.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the file creation and SHA-256 hash output. Save as 01-hash-sha256.png

content.part: Part B: Demonstrate the avalanche effect

steps.text: Create a slightly modified file (change one character):
steps.command: echo "Patient: John Smith, DOB: 1985-03-16, MRN: 12345" > patient2.txt

whatThisDoes.output: Compare the two hashes carefully. Despite changing only one character, the hashes are completely different. This is the avalanche effect — a critical property of cryptographic hash functions. If small changes produced similar hashes, attackers could predict modifications. The avalanche effect makes that impossible.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture both hashes side-by-side showing completely different outputs despite minimal input change. Save as 02-hash-comparison.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Using MD5 or SHA-1: These are deprecated for security purposes — always use SHA-256 or stronger
Confusing hashing with encryption: Hashes are one-way; you cannot reverse them to get original data

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Two screenshots showing SHA-256 hash generation and the avalanche effect. The hashes should be completely different despite only one character changing in the input.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 6 covers cryptographic hash functions and their security properties. Pay attention to collision resistance and why MD5/SHA-1 are deprecated.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What is key length and why does it matter? Research how long it would take to brute-force AES-128 versus AES-256 with current technology.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: NIST SP 800-57 - Recommendation for Key Management. Available at: https://csrc.nist.gov/publications/detail/sp/800-57-part-1/rev-5/final

content.whatYoureDoing: Encrypting and decrypting files using AES-256, the symmetric encryption standard required by HIPAA for protecting patient health information (PHI) at rest.
content.whyItMatters: Symmetric encryption is how organizations protect bulk data. Every encrypted database, every secure file transfer, every protected backup uses symmetric encryption. AES-256 specifically is the gold standard — it’s approved for classified government information and required by most healthcare compliance frameworks.

content.title: Security Compliance Specialist, NEXUS Security
content.quote: HIPAA requires encryption of PHI at rest. AES-256 is the standard. Know it cold.

content.part: Part A: Encrypt a file with AES-256

steps.text: Encrypt your patient file (use password nexus2026 when prompted):

whatThisDoes.concept: This encrypts a file using AES-256 in CBC mode — the same algorithm the U.S. government uses for classified information. Symmetric encryption means the same password both encrypts and decrypts. HIPAA requires AES-256 for protecting patient health information (PHI) at rest.
whatThisDoes.output: OpenSSL will prompt for a password twice (enter and verify). Use nexus2026 as instructed. No output means success — OpenSSL is silent on success.

flags.description: Algorithm: AES with 256-bit key in Cipher Block Chaining mode
Adds random data so the same password + file produces different ciphertext each time
Uses PBKDF2 key derivation — makes brute-force password attacks much slower
Input plaintext file and output encrypted file

steps.text: View the encrypted contents as a hex dump:

whatThisDoes.concept: xxd is a hex dump utility that shows raw file bytes in hexadecimal format. Piping to head limits output to the first 10 lines. This lets you visually confirm the file is encrypted — you should see random-looking hex values, not readable text.
whatThisDoes.output: The hex dump should show no recognizable text — just seemingly random bytes. The first 16 bytes contain the Salted__ header followed by the salt value. If you can read any of the original patient data, encryption failed.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the encryption command and the hex dump showing unreadable encrypted content. Save as 03-symmetric-encrypt.png

whatThisDoes.concept: The -d flag switches OpenSSL to decrypt mode — this single flag reverses the encryption operation. Everything else stays the same: same algorithm, same key derivation. This demonstrates that symmetric encryption is reversible with the correct password.

steps.command: diff patient.txt patient_decrypted.txt && echo 'Files match!' || echo 'Files differ!'

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the decryption and verification showing the original content restored. Save as 04-symmetric-decrypt.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Forgetting the password: Symmetric encryption requires the same password for decryption
Using weak algorithms: DES is obsolete; always use AES-256
Missing -pbkdf2 flag: Modern OpenSSL requires this for key derivation

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Two screenshots showing successful encryption (unreadable ciphertext) and decryption (original plaintext restored).

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 6 covers symmetric encryption algorithms including AES. Understand the difference between block modes (CBC, GCM) and why key management is critical.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What is a digital certificate and how does it establish trust? Research the role of Certificate Authorities in the PKI ecosystem.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: Your textbook Chapter 4 covers PKI and certificate authorities. RFC 5280 defines the X.509 certificate format: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5280

content.whatYoureDoing: Generating an RSA keypair and using it to encrypt and decrypt data. Unlike symmetric encryption (one shared key), asymmetric uses two mathematically related keys — a public key anyone can use to encrypt, and a private key only you possess to decrypt.
content.whyItMatters: Asymmetric encryption solves the key distribution problem. How do you securely share a symmetric key with someone you’ve never met? You use their public key. This is how TLS/HTTPS works, how secure email works, and how certificates establish trust.

content.title: Security Compliance Specialist, NEXUS Security
content.quote: Encryption is not magic. It’s math. And math done wrong is worse than no math at all — it gives false confidence.

content.part: Part A: Generate an RSA keypair

whatThisDoes.concept: This generates an RSA private key — the mathematical foundation for asymmetric cryptography. Unlike symmetric encryption (one shared secret), RSA uses a keypair: a private key you guard with your life, and a public key you share freely. The security comes from the mathematical difficulty of factoring large prime numbers.

flags.description: Save to PEM format (Base64-encoded, human-readable headers)
Key size in bits — NIST minimum for RSA. 4096 is more future-proof but slower.

whatThisDoes.concept: The public key is mathematically derived from the private key — but the reverse is computationally infeasible. This one-way relationship is what makes asymmetric crypto work: you can freely distribute your public key without exposing your private key. Anyone with your public key can encrypt messages that only your private key can decrypt.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the keypair generation, file listing, and public key contents. Save as 05-rsa-keypair.png

content.part: Part B: Encrypt with public key

steps.text: Create a small message to encrypt:
steps.command: echo "Secret: AES key is nexus2026" > secret.txt

whatThisDoes.concept: Public key encryption — anyone with your public key can encrypt a message, but only the private key holder can decrypt it. This solves the key distribution problem: you never need to share a secret password over an insecure channel.

flags.description: Public key utility for encrypt/decrypt/sign/verify operations
Input key is a public key (not a private key)
Path to the key file to use

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the encryption command and verify the encrypted file exists. Save as 06-rsa-encrypt.png

content.part: Part C: Decrypt with private key

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the decryption showing the original secret message restored. Save as 07-rsa-decrypt.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Confusing public/private: Encrypt with public, decrypt with private (for confidentiality)
Data size limits: RSA can only encrypt small amounts of data directly
Exposing private key: Never share or commit private keys

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Three screenshots showing keypair generation, public key encryption, and private key decryption.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 6 covers asymmetric cryptography including RSA. Understand why we use asymmetric crypto for key exchange but symmetric for bulk data.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: How does OpenSSL implement encryption algorithms? Research the difference between block ciphers and stream ciphers.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: OpenSSL Documentation - Cryptographic Algorithms. Available at: https://www.openssl.org/docs/

content.whatYoureDoing: Creating and verifying digital signatures using RSA. Digital signatures use the private key to sign (proving authenticity) and the public key to verify (confirming identity). This is the inverse of encryption.
content.whyItMatters: Digital signatures provide non-repudiation — proof that a specific person signed a specific document. In healthcare, this is legally binding. When a physician signs an order electronically, that signature must be verifiable and undeniable.

content.title: Security Compliance Specialist, NEXUS Security
content.quote: Non-repudiation matters in healthcare. When a provider signs an order, it’s legally binding.

steps.command: echo "Medication Order: Amoxicillin 500mg TID x 10 days - Dr. Chen" > order.txt

whatThisDoes.concept: Digital signing is encryption in reverse — you sign with your private key, and anyone can verify with your public key. This provides non-repudiation: proof that a specific person signed a specific document. In healthcare, this is how electronic prescriptions and medical orders are legally binding.

flags.description: Hash the document with SHA-256 before signing
Sign using your private key — only you can create this signature
Save the binary signature to a separate file

steps.text: Verify the signature file was created:

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the signing command and file listing showing both the order and signature files. Save as 08-signature-create.png

whatThisDoes.output: You should see Verified OK — this confirms three things simultaneously: (1) the document has not been modified since signing, (2) the signature was created by the holder of the matching private key, and (3) the signer cannot deny having signed it (non-repudiation). If even one byte of order.txt changed, verification would fail.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the verification showing “Verified OK” message. Save as 09-signature-verify.png — this is your last screenshot for Lab 2.1!

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Confusing sign/verify with encrypt/decrypt: Sign with private, verify with public
Modified document: Any change to the document will cause verification to fail

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Two screenshots showing signature creation and successful verification with “Verified OK” output.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 6 covers digital signatures and non-repudiation. Understand how signatures combine hashing with asymmetric crypto to provide both integrity and authenticity.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What makes a hash algorithm ‘cryptographically secure’? Research why MD5 and SHA-1 are considered deprecated for security purposes.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: NIST Policy on Hash Functions. Available at: https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/hash-functions

items.label: Hash Collision Exploitation
items.text: Hash collisions expose weak integrity checks — attackers modify files and regenerate matching hashes to cover their tracks when SHA-1 or MD5 is in use.

items.label: Symmetric Key Theft
items.text: Intercepting or stealing AES keys gives full decryption capability; key management failures are more common than algorithm breaks.

items.label: RSA Key Misuse
items.text: Confusing public and private key roles, or using undersized keys, allows attackers to break encryption or forge signatures on affected systems.

items.label: Hash Algorithm Policy
items.text: Use SHA-256 or stronger for all integrity checks; flag any system still using MD5 or SHA-1 as a compliance finding.

items.label: Key Management Controls
items.text: Enforce key management policies — keys stored at rest must be encrypted, and private keys must never leave the system that generated them.

items.label: Signature Verification
items.text: Verify digital signatures as part of software deployment pipelines — unsigned or unverified binaries are a malware delivery vector.

purpleTeam.connection: You generated keys and signed documents as a builder this week. In Module 4 you will reverse that perspective — analyzing how attackers exploit weak key management and deprecated algorithms to compromise the same systems you are securing here.

Side-by-side hash comparison demonstrating the avalanche effect
AES-256-CBC encryption command and encrypted file listing
RSA keypair generation showing both key files
RSA public-key encryption of test data
RSA private-key decryption confirming original plaintext
Digital signature creation showing signature file

All 9 screenshots saved to Module2_Screenshots folder

finalChecklist.canvasQuiz: Take the Lab 2.1 Quiz in Canvas. Questions will ask about specific outputs you should have seen. Keep your VM running while taking the quiz.

customCallouts.title: Lab Environment Only
customCallouts.text: The keys and passwords you create in this lab are for training purposes only. Never use lab-generated keys in production systems.

needHelp.nextModule: After completing this lab, take the Lab 2.1 Quiz in Canvas, then proceed to Lab 2.2: Authentication Systems where you’ll learn SSH key management, certificate-based authentication, and MFA concepts.
needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late work is not accepted. Drop policies exist for emergencies.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Complete hands-on exercises on your behalf
genAI.principle: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

whatsNext.text: After completing this lab and taking the Lab 2.1 Quiz in Canvas, proceed to Lab 2.2: Authentication Systems. You will build on this week's asymmetric cryptography knowledge to generate SSH keypairs, configure key permissions, and implement multi-factor authentication — skills you'll apply directly when you SSH into the Gulf Coast portal container in Lab 2.3.
